description: Fix five categories of wrong assertions in E2E tests that currently pass vacuously or check the wrong condition, masking real product bugs. After all stories pass: (1) the disabled-tool error test verifies the actual disabled-tool error message, not the unreviewed-plugin message; (2) auth-bootstrap step 3 waits for a meaningful connection-failure signal; (3) reload-coalescing verifies coalescing via reload count, not durationMs; (4) multi-tab-targeting throws when the tab marker is an unexpected value; (5) mcp-session-persistence does not silently swallow unexpected in-flight errors.

userStories.title: Fix wrong error message assertion in side-panel-plugin-toggle
userStories.description: The test at line ~354 in side-panel-plugin-toggle.e2e.ts asserts `disabledResult.content.toContain('has not been reviewed yet')` after setting a tool's individual permission to 'off' on an already-reviewed plugin. The plugin has `permission: 'auto'` with `reviewedVersion` set (lines 160-162), so it IS reviewed. When a tool's individual permission is 'off', the server returns an error about the tool being disabled — not the plugin review error. The assertion checks the wrong error class and will fail or pass for wrong reasons depending on what the server actually returns.
acceptanceCriteria: The assertion at the equivalent of line 354 in e2e/side-panel-plugin-toggle.e2e.ts no longer asserts `'has not been reviewed yet'` for a disabled-tool error
The assertion instead checks that the error content describes the tool being disabled/off (e.g., `'is disabled'` or `'permission'` or the actual error substring returned by the server)
userStories.notes:
Files:
- e2e/side-panel-plugin-toggle.e2e.ts (fix wrong substring in toContain assertion)

Context:
The test sets up a plugin with permission 'auto' and reviewedVersion set (lines 160-162:
```
permissions: { 'e2e-test': { permission: 'auto', reviewedVersion: pluginVersion } },
```
Then via the side panel UI it sets the echo tool's individual permission to 'off' (line 333). It then calls the tool via MCP and asserts isError:true plus checks the error message. Currently the assertion is:
```
expect(disabledResult.content).toContain('has not been reviewed yet');
```
But the plugin IS reviewed (reviewedVersion is set), so the error should be about the tool being disabled, not about review.

Changes:
Read the actual error message returned by the server when a tool-level permission is 'off' on an already-reviewed plugin. The off-tool error for a per-tool override is different from the plugin-level review error. Check platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-tool-dispatch.ts or mcp-setup.ts for the actual error string produced when tool permission is 'off'. Then update the assertion to match the actual error text.

Constraints:
- Do NOT change the plugin permission setup (plugin stays at 'auto', only echo tool is set to 'off')
- Do NOT remove the `isError === true` assertion
- The test should verify the error message is about being disabled, not about needing review

Trap:
Do NOT just replace 'has not been reviewed yet' with an empty string or remove the check. The assertion should verify a meaningful substring from the actual disabled-tool error message returned by the server. Read the server source to find the actual string.

userStories.title: Replace trivially-true poll in auth-bootstrap with meaningful wait
userStories.description: In auth-bootstrap.e2e.ts, step 3 uses `expect.poll(...).toBe(false)` on `h?.extensionConnected`. Since `extensionConnected` starts as `false` (no extension has connected yet), this assertion trivially passes on the very first poll tick — it does not wait for the extension to actually attempt and fail a connection. The intent is to confirm the extension has tried to connect (and failed because auth.json doesn't exist) before writing auth.json at step 5. The 10-second wait does nothing meaningful.
acceptanceCriteria: Step 3 in auth-bootstrap.e2e.ts waits for an observable signal that the extension has attempted connection and failed — not just that it hasn't connected yet

The assertion can distinguish 'never tried' from 'tried and failed' (e.g., by polling for a log line, a health field, or waiting a fixed but reasonable duration long enough for the extension to attempt connection)
The subsequent steps 5-8 (write auth.json, waitForExtensionConnected, tool dispatch) remain unchanged
userStories.notes:
Files:
- e2e/auth-bootstrap.e2e.ts (replace trivially-true poll at lines ~55-63)

Context:
The test starts an MCP server (step 1), launches the extension WITHOUT auth.json (step 2), then step 3 asserts `extensionConnected === false`:
```ts
await expect
  .poll(
    async () => {
      const h = await server?.health();
      return h?.extensionConnected;
    },
    { timeout: 10_000, message: 'extension should not connect without auth.json' },
  )
  .toBe(false);
```
Since extensionConnected is false from the start, this passes immediately. The point is to ensure the extension is in its connection-retry loop (having tried and failed) before writing auth.json.

Changes:
Replace the trivially-true poll with a meaningful wait. Options:
1. Wait for a log entry indicating the extension attempted connection (check what the server logs when /ws-info returns 401 or when a WS connection is rejected due to missing secret)
2. Use a minimal fixed wait (e.g., `await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 3_000))`) — less elegant but at least waits for the extension to have tried
3. Poll for an observable health field that indicates a connection was attempted (if such a field exists, e.g., a connection attempt counter)

Read platform/mcp-server/src/index.ts or extension-protocol.ts for what gets logged when a connection fails authentication. Also check e2e/fixtures.ts `waitForLog` helper.

Constraints:
- Do NOT change the overall test structure (steps 1-8 as documented)
- The test must still proceed to write auth.json AFTER verifying the failed state

Trap:
Do NOT just remove the assertion. The test needs SOME wait here to ensure the extension is in retry mode. A simple `waitForLog(server, '<auth-failure-message>', 15_000)` is the cleanest solution if such a log exists.

userStories.title: Fix vacuous coalescing assertion in reload-coalescing and stale waitForLog in browser-permission-persist
userStories.description: Two test files have assertions that can pass trivially without verifying the intended behavior. (1) reload-coalescing.e2e.ts line ~62 asserts `new Set(bodies.map(b => b.durationMs)).size === 1` to verify all 5 concurrent reloads shared a single coalesced cycle. But if plugin discovery takes 0ms (or all reloads happen to have the same duration), the set size is 1 regardless of coalescing. (2) browser-permission-persist.e2e.ts line ~89 calls `waitForLog(server, 'Config reload complete', 10_000)` a second time after a second POST /reload, but `waitForLog` scans the full accumulated log buffer and finds the first reload's entry immediately — it never waits for the second reload to complete.
acceptanceCriteria: In reload-coalescing.e2e.ts, the coalescing assertion verifies a discriminator that is unique per reload cycle — e.g., checking that only one 'Hot reload complete' log entry was emitted across all 5 concurrent requests, or that the /health reloadCount only incremented by 1

In browser-permission-persist.e2e.ts, the second `waitForLog('Config reload complete')` call after the second POST /reload is replaced with a wait that confirms the SECOND reload specifically completed (e.g., by counting occurrences, clearing logs before the second reload, or using a health-endpoint predicate)
userStories.notes:
Files:
- e2e/reload-coalescing.e2e.ts (fix durationMs-based coalescing check, ~lines 41-66)
- e2e/browser-permission-persist.e2e.ts (fix stale waitForLog at ~line 89)

Context for reload-coalescing.e2e.ts:
The test fires 5 concurrent POST /reload requests and asserts they were coalesced into one reload:
```ts
const durations = new Set(bodies.map(b => b.durationMs));
expect(durations.size).toBe(1);
```
This is fragile — if the discovery is instantaneous (durationMs=0 for all), the set is trivially size 1. Better check: after the 5 concurrent reloads complete, verify the server's `reloadCount` only increased by 1. The /health endpoint includes `reloadCount`. Fetch /health before and after and assert `healthAfter.reloadCount === healthBefore.reloadCount + 1`.

Context for browser-permission-persist.e2e.ts:
`waitForLog` in helpers.ts does: `server.logs.join('\n').includes(substring)`. After the first reload, `server.logs` already contains 'Config reload complete'. The second `waitForLog` at line ~89 returns immediately on the stale entry:
```ts
const reloadResp2 = await postReload(server.port, configDir);
expect(reloadResp2.ok).toBe(true);
await waitForLog(server, 'Config reload complete', 10_000); // returns immediately!
```
Fix: clear `server.logs.length = 0` BEFORE the second postReload call (not after), then call `waitForLog`. OR count occurrences: `server.logs.filter(l => l.includes('Config reload complete')).length >= 2`. OR use `waitForHealth(h => h.reloadCount === initialCount + 2)` to wait for 2 reloads to complete.

Constraints:
- Do NOT remove the wait — the test needs to verify the reload completed before checking disk state
- The fix in browser-permission-persist must ensure the SECOND reload is confirmed before asserting disk state at line ~92

userStories.title: Fix silent tab ID swap in multi-tab-targeting when marker is unexpected
userStories.description: In multi-tab-targeting.e2e.ts, after two tabs are opened and `plugin_list_tabs` returns their IDs, the test reads `__tabMarker` from the first tab to identify which tab is 'tab-one' vs 'tab-two'. The identification logic at lines ~159-164 silently falls through to the else branch if `marker1Value` is neither 'tab-one' nor 'tab-two' (e.g., undefined due to a timing issue), silently swapping the tab IDs. All subsequent targeted-dispatch assertions then verify the WRONG tabs and can pass despite incorrect routing. The same silent-swap pattern appears at lines ~606-611 and ~886-890.
acceptanceCriteria: In all three occurrences (~lines 159-164, 606-611, 886-890), if `marker1Value` is neither 'tab-one' nor 'tab-two', the test throws an explicit error rather than silently proceeding with swapped IDs
The error message identifies the unexpected marker value to aid debugging

userStories.title: Fix silent catch swallowing unexpected errors in mcp-session-persistence
userStories.description: In mcp-session-persistence.e2e.ts, the in-flight tool call result during hot reload uses a bare `catch {}` that ignores ALL errors (lines ~570-578). The intent is to handle expected errors (502, connection reset) during worker restart. But any unexpected error — a timeout, a programming error, a transport bug — is also silently swallowed, allowing the test to produce a false pass. The catch must be narrowed to only suppress errors that are expected during a worker restart.
acceptanceCriteria: The catch block in the hot-reload in-flight test (~lines 570-578 in e2e/mcp-session-persistence.e2e.ts) no longer swallows all errors
Errors that are clearly from the hot-reload disruption (HTTP 502/503, connection reset, 'missing session' errors) are suppressed
All other unexpected errors are re-thrown so the test fails with a meaningful error message
